abstract: Text classification is a fundamental task in natural language processing, traditionally reliant on manual feature engineering through the use of dictionaries, knowledge bases, and complex kernels. In this paper, we propose a Recurrent Convolutional Neural Network (RCNN) that eliminates the need for human-designed features by leveraging the strengths of both recurrent and convolutional architectures. Our model employs a bi-directional recurrent structure to capture extensive contextual information during the learning of word representations, resulting in significantly less noise compared to standard window-based neural networks. Additionally, we integrate a max-pooling layer to automatically identify and extract the most salient features within a text. Experimental evaluations on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showing particularly strong performance in document-level classification tasks. The model's linear time complexity further ensures efficiency across varyingly sized inputs.
